content: WebSocket security pitfalls for remote terminal: (1) CSWSH (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking) — browsers send cookies on WS upgrade; no CORS equivalent; attacker site can open WS to your server with victim's cookies; mitigation: validate Origin header server-side + CSRF token in upgrade; (2) Authentication timing: accepting WS connection before validating token allows unauthenticated connection flooding (DoS); validate token during HTTP upgrade phase before connection is accepted; (3) xterm.js exposes full shell to any JavaScript on the host page — XSS on the dashboard page = arbitrary terminal commands; (4) Origin header can be spoofed by non-browser clients (daemon); daemon must use token-based auth in upgrade headers, not rely on Origin; (5) No per-message authentication after connection established — if session expires mid-connection, commands continue executing; need periodic session revalidation; (6) Message size limits needed to prevent payload flooding; (7) WS connections are stateful — serverless deployment requires sticky sessions or external session store.
